# Intercalation-Activated Layered MoO3Nanobelts as Biodegradable Nanozymes for Tumor-Specific Photo-Enhanced Catalytic Therapy


Zhan Zhou + , Yanlong Wang + , Feng Peng + , Fanqi Meng + , Jiajia Zha, Lu Ma, Yonghua Du, Na Peng, Lufang Ma, Qinghua Zhang, Lin Gu,* Wenyan Yin,* Zhanjun Gu,* and Chaoliang Tan*

Abstract: The existence of natural van der Waals gaps in layered materials allows them to be easily intercalated with varying guest species, offering an appealing strategy to optimize their physicochemical properties and application performance. Herein, we report the activation of layered MoO3nanobelts via aqueous intercalation as an efficient biodegradable nanozyme for tumor-specific photo-enhanced catalytic therapy. The long MoO3nanobelts are grinded and then intercalated with Na + and H2O to obtain the short Na + /H2O co-intercalated MoO3GLYPH<0> x (NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x ) nanobelts. In contrast to the inert MoO3nanobelts, the NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x nanobelts exhibit excellent enzyme-mimicking catalytic activity for generation of reactive oxygen species, which can be further enhanced by the photothermal effect under a1064nm laser irradiation. Thus, after bovine serum albumin modification, the NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x nanobelts can efficiently kill cancer cells in vitro and eliminate tumors in vivo facilitating with1064nm laser irradiation.

# Introduction


Layered materials are a class of functional materials with highly anisotropic bonding, in which each layer is strongly bonded via covalent bonding and adjacent layers are only weakly bonded via van der Waals interactions. [1-3] One of the unique advantages for layered materials is the existence of natural van der Waals gaps, allowing them to be easily intercalated with varying guest species, such as cations, anions, organic molecules and conductive polymers. [4-6] By taking this advantage, intercalation has been developed as one of the most appealing strategies to tune/optimize the physical, chemical and electronic properties of layered materials and/or boost their performance in various promising applications. [7-9] For example, organic cation intercalation has been proposed as a fascinating approach to remarkably boost the thermoelectric and electronic device performance of layered TiS2and black phosphorus, respectively. [10,11] Furthermore, the pre-intercalation of metal cations (e.g., Na + , Ca2 + and Mg2 + ) or conducting polymers

(e.g., polyaniline) into layered metal oxides, such as MoO3, MnO2and V2O5, can expand their interlayer spacings, regulate their electronic properties and/or generate rich oxygen vacancies and thus could dramatically enhance their performance in rechargeable batteries, including Li-ion, Naion and Zinc-ion batteries. [12-19] Although the intercalation has been widely used as a promising strategy for structural engineering of layered materials, they mainly focused on the optimization of their performance in thermoelectric devices, electronic devices and rechargeable batteries. Therefore, how the intercalation affects the performance of layered nanomaterials for biomedical applications, such as photothermal and catalytic therapy, still remain unexplored.

Nanomaterials with enzyme-mimicking oxidoreductase activities including peroxidase (POD), oxidase (OXD), and catalase (CAT), so-called 'nanozymes', have been one of central topics in nanomedicine in the last decade since they can provide an ideal platform to realize highly efficient tumor diagnostic and/or therapeutic outcomes. [20-24] One of the major concerns for nanozymes is that their undesired off-target activity leads to unpredictable toxicity. [25,26] Hence, great effort has been devoted to the development of tumor microenvironment (TME)-responsive nanozymes that only exhibit catalytic reaction-based therapeutic effects, while minimum side effects in normal tissues. [27-29] Promisingly, recent studies have demonstrated that some nanozymes exhibit TME-responsive (e.g., H2O2or pH-responsive) enzyme-mimicking catalytic activity and thus can achieve tumor-specific therapy since the microenvironment in tumors, such as the high H2O2concentration and acidic pH value, is different from that of in normal tissues. [30-33] Among them, molybdenum oxide nanomaterials have been proven to be efficient nanozymes for tumor-specific catalytic therapy because of their low toxicity, variable oxidation states of Mo ions and excellent biodegradability. [34-38] For example, MoO3GLYPH<0> x nanourchins and Fe-MoOv nanoparticles have been developed as efficient nanozymes for tumorspecific catalytic therapy and the structural engineering, including morphology tuning or defect engineering, has be proven to be essential to regulate their enzyme-mimicking catalytic activity. [36,37] However, the current reported molybdenum oxide-based nanozymes still suffer from the relatively low catalytic activity or need to introduce additional active metal elements. Therefore, it is quite urgent to develop simple but effective strategies to prepare highly active redox nanozymes that are competitive to natural enzymes for catalytic therapy.

In this contribution, we report the intercalation as a promising strategy to activate the enzyme-mimicking catalytic activity of layered MoO3nanobelts, making it an efficient biodegradable nanozyme for tumor-specific photoenhanced catalytic therapy. The hydrothermal-synthesized micrometer-long MoO3nanobelts are ball-milled and then co-intercalated with Na + and H2O via aqueous solution intercalation to obtain the dark-blue co-intercalated MoO3GLYPH<0> x (denoted as NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x ) nanobelts (Figure1). Importantly, the co-intercalation not only can partially reduce the Mo6 + to Mo5 + and generate rich defects on the MoO3nanobelts, but also can boost its absorption in the near-infrared (NIR)-

II region because of the band gap narrowing. Thus, the NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x nanobelts exhibit excellent enzyme-mimicking catalytic activity for generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) including * OH and * O2GLYPH<0> in the TME in comparison with the inert MoO3nanobelts (Figure1). Moreover, the enzyme-mimicking catalytic activity of NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x nanobelts can be further enhanced by the photothermal effect under a1064nm laser irradiation. Thus, after modification with bovine serum albumin (BSA), the NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x @BSA nanobelts exhibit remarkable tumor ablation when used as a nanozyme for the photo-driven tumor-specific catalytic therapy (Figure1). Interestingly, the NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x @BSA nanobelts are able to degrade rapidly and be excreted from the mice body, minimizing the in vivo toxicity for normal organs. This work provides a promising example on activating layered MoO3nanomaterials via intercalation as nanozymes for highly efficient tumor-specific catalytic therapy.


# Results and Discussion


Single-crystalline MoO3nanobelts with micrometer lengths were prepared by a hydrothermal approach according to the previously reported method. [39] The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of the MoO3nanobelts is shown in Figure2a and all the peaks match well with the standard reference of the layered orthorhombic MoO3 ( α -MoO3) (PDF05-0508), confirming its crystal structure. As shown in the scanning electron microscope (SEM) image (Figure2b), the prepared MoO3nanobelts have a length up to5-10 μm and a width of hundreds of nanometers. The morphology and crystal structure of the MoO3nanobelts were further verified by transmission electron microscope (TEM) characterization (Figure S1). In order to shorten its length, the micrometerlong MoO3nanobelts were grinded for2h via a dry ball milling process to obtain short MoO3nanobelts (denoted as MoO3-BM). As shown Figure2c, the SEM image shows that the MoO3-BM nanobelts have a much shorter length, which is around50-200nm. The crystal structure of the MoO3-BM nanobelts maintain well, which is evidenced by the scanning transmission electron microscope (STEM) and its corresponding atomic resolution STEM images (Figure S2). As shown in Figure S3, the XRD pattern of MoO3-BM nanobelts matches well with the standard reference of the α -MoO3crystal (PDF05-0508). All the aforementioned characterization results suggest that the balling milling mainly shortens the length of MoO3nanobelts.

The MoO3-BM nanobelts were then intercalated with Na + and H2O by reacting with sodium molybdate and sodium dithionite in aqueous solution to obtain the NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x nanobelts. [40] As shown in the XRD pattern, all the XRD peaks of the intercalated sample match well with the simulated peaks based on the crystal structure of hydrated molybdenum bronze [Na(H2O)2]0.25MoO3 (Figure2a). [40] This hydrated molybdenum bronze can be considered as the layered α -MoO3co-intercalated with Na + and H2O. It is worth pointing out that the XRD peak corresponding to the (020) planes of the NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x

The peak position of (020) planes in the XRD pattern indicates the interlayer distancing of layered α -MoO3. The obvious shift of this peak to lower angle suggests the expansion of the interlayer distancing of NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x nanobelts induced by the cointercalation with Na + and H2O.

The SEM image clearly shows that the NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x nanobelts have a uniform size of100-300nm (Figure2d). The STEM image of the NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x nanobelts (Figure2e) displays a similar result with the SEM image. A continuous lattice fringe can be seen from the high-resolution STEM image (Figure2f) of a typical NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x nanobelt (inset in Figure2f), indicating its single-crystalline characteristic. The measured lattice distance is � 0.38nm, which is assignable to the (100) planes of the [Na(H2O)2]0.25MoO3crystal. The Figure2g shows the atomic resolution STEM image of MoO3nanobelt viewing from y-axis, suggesting that the MoO3nanobelt a layered structure and singlecrystalline nature. The measured interlayer distancing is � 0.72nm (Figure2g), which is corresponding to the (020) planes of the layered α -MoO3crystal. Similarly, the atomic resolution STEM image of a typical NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x nanobelt viewing from y-axis also displays its layered structure (Figure2h). However, the measured interlayer distancing for the (020) planes is � 0.87nm (Figure2h), which is much larger than that of layered α -MoO3 (i.e. 0.72nm). Such results clearly prove the obvious expansion of the interlayer distancing of layered NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x nanobelts as compared to the layered α -MoO3nanobelts, which is consistent with the XRD results. It is worth pointing out that not all the interlayer spacings are intercalated with Na + and H2O since some parts of interlayer distancing remain the same as the layered α -MoO3 (Figure2h). Meanwhile, some defects (indicating by dash circles) can be also seen from the atomic resolution STEM image (Figure2h), suggesting that the intercalation process also gives rise to some defects on the NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x nanobelts.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to further characterize the structural changes of MoO3nanobelts caused by the co-intercalation. The high-resolution XPS Mo3d of the MoO3and NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x nanobelts are shown in Figure3a. As shown in Figure3a, the XPS Mo3d spectrum of the MoO3nanobelts gives two peaks at236.0and232.9eV, which is attributed to the Mo6 + of MoO3. [41,42] In contrast, the XPS Mo3d spectrum of the NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x presents two shoulder peaks at234.7and231.6eV, assignable to the Mo5 + in the NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x nanobelts, in addition to the two main peaks at236.0and232.9eV. [41,42] Such a result indicates that the Mo atoms were partially reduced from Mo6 + to Mo5 + during the intercalation process. According to the deconvolution of the high-resolution XPS

Similarly, a new shoulder peak at531.4eV can be observed in the XPS O1s spectrum of the NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x nanobelts in addition to the main peak at530.7eV in comparison with the XPS O1s spectrum of the MoO3 (Figure3b), which might be due to oxygen vacancies caused by the partial reduction. [41,42] Importantly, in contract the non-detectable peak in XPS Na1s spectrum of the MoO3nanobelts (Figure3c), an obvious peak at1071.8eV corresponding to the Na + can be detected in XPS Na1s spectrum of the NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x nanobelts, confirming the presence of Na + in the NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x nano- belts. The MoO3and NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x nanobelts were also characterized by electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectroscopy. As shown in Figure3d, both of the MoO3and NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x nanobelts display peaks at g values of2.0and1.91, which are corresponding to the signals from oxygen vacancies and the Mo5 + , respectively. [43] It can be clearly observed that the NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x nanobelts give much stronger intensity for both peaks than that of the MoO3nanobelts, suggesting that much more oxygen vacancies and Mo5 + ions in the NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x nanobelts than those of the MoO3nanobelts. [43] The EPR results are consistent with analysis from other characterizations, including TEM and XPS. Furthermore, both of the MoO3and NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x

nanobelts were characterized by the Mo K-edge X-ray absorption near-edge structure (XANES) spectra. The radial atomic distribution around Mo atoms for both of the samples are evidenced by the Fourier transformed extended X-ray absorption fine structure (EXAFS) spectra (Figure3e). Note that the NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x nanobelts show slightly peak position shift and much lower peak intensity as compared to the MoO3nanobelts (Figure3e). The much lower peak intensity suggests the significantly increased structure distortion and the peak position shift indicates the slightly changes on the bond lengths around Mo atoms, which can be attributed to the partial reduction of the Mo atoms and crystal structure induced by the intercalation, respectively. [44]

As shown in Figure3f, the Raman spectrum of the NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x nanobelts gives different characteristic peaks with much weaker intensity as compared to that of the MoO3nanobelts. [39] In addition, a few new characteristic peaks can be observed between850-1000cm GLYPH<0> 1 . The Raman spectrum change should be originated from the intercalation. Therefore, it was found that multiple structural changes can be achieved by the simple co-intercalation with Na + and H2O into layered MoO3nanobelts, including partially reducing Mo6 + to Mo5 + , expanding the interlayer gaps, generating rich defects (e.g. oxygen vacancies) and changing crystal structure, to obtain short, interlayer gap-expanded and intercalated NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x nanobelts.

The intercalation process can also give rise to a color change on the MoO3nanobelts from white to dark blue. As shown in Figure4a, the photographs of powders and solutions of NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x nanobelts show an obvious dark blue color, distinctive from the white color of the MoO3nanobelts. Therefore, the UV/Vis-NIR absorption of the MoO3, MoO3-BM, and NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x nanobelts are measured at the same concentration (100ppm). The MoO3nanobelts exhibit weak absorption peaks in the range of400to1300nm and its absorption intensity gradually decreased could be attributed to light scattering induced by its micrometer-long length (Figure4b). Note that the short MoO3BM nanobelts show negligible absorption intensity after600nm since the light scattering no longer exists because of the reduced size to hundreds of nanometers (Figure4b). Interestingly, the absorption of NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x nanobelts gradually increase along with the wavelength, resulting in strong absorption in the NIR-II window (Figure4b), which is consistent with the UV/Vis-NIR spectra calculated by density functional theory (DFT) (Figure4c). In order to figure out the mechanism behind the absorption change, the electronic band structure and optical property were simulated by first principles DFT. We can find that the MoO3has a band gap of2.592eV, in which the valence band maximum (VBM) located at the high symmetry U point and the conduction band minimum (CBM) located at the X point (Figure4d). NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x has a band gap of1.793eV (Figure4e) which decreases0.8eV compared with the pristine MoO3. It can be seen from the band structure that the conduction band of the NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x also moves downward to the Fermi level as compared to the pristine MoO3, since one Na atom was introduced in the MoO3crystal, which leads to a further narrowing of the band gap. Furthermore, as shown in the electronic energy band of NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x , defect energy levels appear between the conduction band and the valence band from (Figure4e). In this case, the defect energy levels could facilitate the electron transfer and the valence electrons could transit upward to the defect level first and then the conduction band. Hence, it is reasonable that the MoO3nanobelts have negligible absorption after600nm since it has a large band of � 2.592eV. As for the NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x nanobelts, the broad absorption band from visible to NIR region possibly is caused its much smaller band gap and defect energy level-enabled electron transition.

The UV/Vis-NIR absorption intensity of the obtained NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x nanobelts increased gradually from visible light to NIR region (especially in NIR-II region), which indicates that it has the potential to be used as a photothermal agent under a NIR laser radiation. As shown in the concentrationdependent absorption of NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x nanobelts (Figure S4), its mass extinction coefficient at NIR-II (1064nm) was calculated to be6.7Lg GLYPH<0> 1cm GLYPH<0> 1 . To promote the biocompatibility of inorganic nanomaterials, it is necessary to modify their surfaces with polymers (e.g., polyvinylpyrro- lidone (PVP), polyethylene glycol (PEG) or polyvinyl alcohol (PVA)) or biological macromolecules (e.g., glutathione (GSH), hyaluronic acid (HA) or BSA). Therefore, a natural biological macromolecule, i.e., BSA, was used to modify the NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x nanobelts to get the BSA-modified NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x nanobelts (NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x @BSA). Then, the photothermal effect was studied by the continuous irradiation of DI water solution containing NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x @BSA with various concentrations using a NIR-II laser (1064nm) for10min at a power density of1.0Wcm GLYPH<0> 2 . As shown in Figure4f, the NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x @BSA displays concentrationdependent heating effect under the NIR-II laser irradiation, and the water solution is heated from25 ° C to58.3 ° C by adjusting its concentration in contrast to the slight temperature changes (from25 ° C to33.8 ° C) for the pure DI water, indicating that NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x @BSA could rapidly covert NIR light energy to the thermal energy. The solution of NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x @BSA (200ppm) was further irradiated with various laser power densities, and the heating curves displayed the amplitude of heating increased with the increase of laser power density (Figure S5a), demonstrating the laser power-dependent photothermal properties of NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x @BSA. Moreover, the good photostability of NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x @BSA was confirmed by the slight changes in its photothermal effect after five laser power on/off cycles (Figure S5b). The photothermal conversion efficiency of NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x @BSA was calculated to be24.27% based on its heating-cooling curve (Figure S5c,d), indicating that

NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x @BSA could effectively covert light energy to thermal energy.

The abundant oxygen vacancies, Mo5 + , and appropriate band gap of NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x nanobelts makes it possible to be a nanozyme catalyst. After adding NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x @BSA into a specific concentration of H2O2solution, H2O2was dramatically decomposed in a time-dependent manner under the neutral or weak acidic condition (Figure S6). The POD-like activity of NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x @BSA was investigated by reducing H2O2to * OH that could be monitored by the traditional3,3 ' ,5,5 ' -tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) substrate. NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x @BSA presented significant POD-like activity (Figure5a) in the HAc-NaAc buffer solution (pH6.5), whereas almost no obvious catalytic activity was observed in the typical MoO3nanobelt (Figure S7) at the same conditions, which further proves that the POD-like activity of molybdenum oxide could be activated by intercalation.

When NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x @BSA was irradiated by the1064nm NIR-II laser, its catalytic performance was improved to a certain extent in the buffer solution of pH6.5 (Figure5b), which might be due to the high temperature of the system to facilitate the nanozyme catalytic reaction. We further tested the temperature-dependent POD-like activity of NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x @BSA at different temperature (Figure5c), and its optimal temperature ranged from35to45 ° C. Moreover, the pH-dependent POD-like activity of NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x @BSA was also evaluated in different pH values at37 ° C, and its optimal pH ranged from4.5to6.0 (Figure5d and Figure S8a). The NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x @BSA still maintained superior POD-like activity in the presence of low concentration H2O2 (Figure5e and Figure S8b), and the UV/Vis absorbance intensity at652nm (the characteristic peak for blue oxTMB) versus various concentrations (from0to0.5mM) of H2O2exhibited a good linearity with

Besides * OH, O2can be produced by decomposing H2O2under the catalysis of biological enzyme including catalase (CAT). [46] To investigate the CAT-like activity of NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x @BSA, its oxygen production capacity was measured in the presence of H2O2under different pH value buffer solutions. As shown in Figure5g, the continuous oxygen can be detected after adding NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x @BSA, and the yield of oxygen in weak acidic condition (mimicking TME) is obviously higher than that in neutral physiological environment, indicating NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x @BSA can be used as a CAT-like nanozyme for decomposing intratumoral overexpressed H2O2to produce O2. Compared with NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x @BSA, negligible CAT-like activity was observed for MoO3even under weak acidic condition (Figure S10a), demonstrating the CAT-like activity of molybdenum oxide could also be activated by the intercalation of Na + . It is worth pointing out that CAT-like performance of the NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x @BSA could be improved under a NIR-II laser (1064nm) irradiation (Figure5h), contributing to the photothermal-enhanced catalytic effect. More importantly, the generated O2was further catalyzed by NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x @BSA to form superoxide free radical ( * O2GLYPH<0> ), which was proved by the electron spin resonance (ESR) assay. Only significant signal of * O2GLYPH<0> was detected for the NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x @BSA-catalyzed reaction (Figure5i), indicating that NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x @BSA had superior OXD-like activity to produce * O2GLYPH<0> in comparison to MoO3nanobelts (Figure S10b). [47] All the aforementioned results proved that the NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x @BSA nanobelts possess the multi-enzyme-like (POD, CAT, and OXD) property, and these integrated cascade catalytic reactions could effectively enhance the production of ROS ( * OH and * O2GLYPH<0> ) for catalytic therapy, which can be further accelerated by the photothermal effect under1064nm laser irradiation.

We believed that intercalation induced structural engineering, including partial reduction of Mo6 + , defect generation and interlayer spacing expansion, is attributed to the activation of the NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x nanobelts. Previous studies have demonstrated that metal oxides with defects (e.g. oxygen vacancies) can effectively capture and activate H2O2to generate * OH and O2, thereby enhancing the catalytic properties of nanozymes. [37,48] In our NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x nanobelts, the rich oxygen defects were formed after co-intercalating with Na + and H2O into layered MoO3nanobelts (Figure2h and3d), which can provide more active sites for the adsorption and activation of H2O2to yield the more * OH and O2. Subsequently, the adsorbed O2was reduced to * O2GLYPH<0> through the charge transfer between Mo V and Mo VI[49]

because of the partially reducing Mo6 + to Mo5 + (25.5%) during the intercalation process. Meanwhile, the interlayer spacing of the NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x nanobelts was also widened by the co-intercalation with Na + and H2O, which can reduce its band gap. The band gap narrowing endows the NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x nanobelts with strong absorption in the NIR-II window (Figure4b,e), thereby enabling its good NIR-II photothermal properties. Temperature is one of the important factors affecting enzyme catalyzed reaction and the enzymecatalyzed reaction will accelerate with the increase of temperature. [50] In our case, the increase of localized temperature induced photothermal effect will accelerate the rate of ROS generation and thus achieve highly efficient catalytic therapy. Therefore, layered MoO3nanobelts can be activated by intercalation, making it a highly active nanozyme for photo-enhanced catalytic therapy.

The in vitro cytotoxicity of NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x @BSA was measured by the CCK-8assay. Almost all the human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVEC) still survive after treating with NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x @BSA for24h even at the high concentration of400ppm (Figure6a), demonstrating that NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x @BSA exhibited negligible cytotoxicity toward the normal cells with low H2O2expression. As for the H2O2high expressed cervical cancer cell line (HeLa) and mouse breast cancer cells (4T1), more than50% of cells could be killed at a concentration of400ppm (Figure6b,c), suggesting that the enzyme-like catalytic activity of NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x @BSA could be activated in the TME, which lead to the production of excess ROS and induce cell apoptosis. Meanwhile, we also investigated the photothermal effect of NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x @BSA for4T1under the NIR-II laser irradiation (1064nm, 0.8Wcm GLYPH<0> 2 ) for10min. The cell viability was highly dependent on the concentration of NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x @BSA after the PTT, and the cell survival rate was significantly lower than that of group without preirradiation by the NIR-II laser (Figure6c), which was indicated PTT could enhance the catalytic activity of NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x @BSA in vitro. Compared with hypoxic condition (Figure S11), the NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x exhibits much higher cell inhibition rate under the normoxia, suggesting that it can be used for catalytic therapy in vitro. Moreover, the photo-enhanced enzyme catalysis using NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x @BSA was further proved by co-staining with Calcein-AM and propidium iodide (PI). The living4T1cells (green color) and dead cells (red color) were distinguished by naked eye with the assistance of a confocal laser scanning microscopy (Figure6d). Promisingly, most of the4T1cells were killed in the presence of NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x @BSA (200ppm) under the irradiation of a NIR-II laser (0.8Wcm GLYPH<0> 2 ) for10min, and this cell mortality was much higher than that of group under the dark (Figure6d), which might be due to the more ROS production stimulated by a NIR-II laser and the photothermal activity of NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x @BSA. The tumor volumes and weight of mice in NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x @BSA group were much lower than those of control and only laser groups (Figure7c-e), indicating that NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x @BSA had a certain enzyme inhibitory effect on tumors without laser. To further evaluate the long-term biosafety of NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x @BSA, its hemolysis experiment was first investigated. As shown in Figure S13, the hemolysis rate of NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x @BSA was comparable to that of PBS control even at high concentration (300ppm), proving it had good biosafety in blood. Secondly, blood samples of mice after different treatments at19th day were utilized for routine blood test analysis (Figure S14) and blood biochemical assay (Figure S15). Most of the cell count parameters were normal in all four groups except for the abnormal increasing number of neutrophils (Figure S14a), monocytes (Figure S14b), lymphocytes (Figure S14c), and leukocytes (Figure S14d) in group1, 2, and3, which was probably attributed to the inflammation caused by the tumor. However, the above abnormal parameters returned to normal values after eliminating the tumors in the group4. Importantly, the amount of alkaline phosphatase (ALP) in the mouse blood was also significantly improved after the treatment of NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x @BSA + laser (Figure S15f). Thirdly, the tissue and organ sections of mice for H&E staining and the distribution of molybdenum ions were investigated in detail. No obvious damage was observed in the NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x @BSA + laser group in comparison with the control group (Figure S16). More importantly, inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) measurement (Figure S17) indicated that the accumulation of molybdenum ions in all organs of mice in NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x @BSA + laser group was extremely low, which was close to that of the PBS control group, demonstrating the NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x @BSA could be degraded in mice and excreted from the body with negligible biotoxicity in vivo.


# Conclusion


In summary, we have reported the activation of layered MoO3nanobelts through aqueous Na + /H2O co-intercalation used as highly active nanozymes for photo-enhanced catalytic therapy. Our results have shown that the intercalation can endow the inert MoO3nanobelts with excellent enzymemimicking catalytic activity towards the enhanced generation of vast ROS under mimic TME. Meanwhile, the cointercalation can also activate the absorption of layered MoO3nanobelts in the NIR region, thus enabling its good photothermal effect under a NIR-II laser irradiation. Importantly, the enzyme-mimicking catalytic activity of the NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x nanobelts can be further enhanced by the NIR-laser-induced photothermal effect. Both the in vitro and in vivo have proven that the NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x nanobelts could serve as a highly active nanozyme for selectively killing cancer cells. Importantly, the NH GLYPH<0> MoO3GLYPH<0> x @BSA nanobelts presented excellent biodegradability and could be easy to be excreted from normal organs with negligible in vivo toxicity. Our study has revealed that aqueous intercalation is a simple and effective strategy to activate layered MoO3for highly efficient photo-enhanced catalytic therapy. We believe that this intercalation strategy could also be an effective way to activate/optimize layered metal oxides for other biomedical applications, such as chemodynamic, photodynamic and sonodynamic therapy. In addition, such an intercalation strategy is expected to be also a powerful approach for activating/tuning other layered materials, such as metal chalcogenides, Mxenes, and layered double hydroxides, as highly active nanozymes towards catalytic therapy.

# Experimental Section Chemicals.


Bovine serum albumin (BSA) was purchased from Beijing Puyihua Technology Co., Ltd. 5,5Dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO, 98%), 5, 5'-dithio bis-(2-nitrobenzoic acid) (DTNB), sodium molybdate dihydrate (99.0%), sodium dithionite (85%) and sodium chloride (99.5%) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Company (China). Nitric acid (70%), hydrochloric acid (37%), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and sodium hydroxide (96%) were purchased by Beijing Chemical Reagent Company. Dulbeco's Modified Eagle's Medium (DMEM) was supplied by the HyClone Company. Hoechst33342, 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), 2′,7′dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) and calmodulin AM-propidium iodide (CAPI) fetal bovine serum (FBS) were obtained from Beyotime Biotechnology.

Preparation  of  α-MoO3nanobelts. The  α-MoO3nanobelts  was  synthesized  by  sodium chloride-assisted hydrothermal method reported in the previous literature. [1]  Na2MoO4·2H2O (15mmol) and NaCl (0.002eq, 0.03mmol) were dissolved in DI water (120mL), then12mL of hydrochloric acid aqueous solution (3M) was added into the above solution slowly. After pouring into a250mL of hydrothermal reactor, the solution was heated to180 °C and kept for24h. After that, the reactor was cooled down to room temperature naturally. Finally, the whit precipitate was washed with DI water for several times until the supernatant was neutral.

Preparation of co-intercalated molybdenum oxide nanobelts (denoted as NH-MoO3-x). 2grams of α-MoO3nanobelt powder and appropriate number of steel balls were added into a stainless steel ball milling tank, and sealed by ball milling (40Hz) for0.5h and stop for0.5h with4cycles to obtain the ball milled α-MoO3 (MoO3-BM). The above MoO3-BM (2g) was added into100mL of DI water, and the mixture was transferred into a round bottom flask sealed with a rubber plug. After deaerating with nitrogen gas for1h, Na2MoO4·2H2O (24g)

and sodium dithionite (0.8g) were poured into the solution at the same time, and stirred at room temperature for another3h under nitrogen protection. The precipitate was washed with DI water thoroughly until the supernatant is colorless, and the blue-color molybdenum oxide nanobelts co-intercalated with Na + and H2O molecules (denoted as NH-MoO3-x for short) was obtained by drying in air at90 °C for overnight.

Surface modification of NH-MoO3-x with BSA. BSA (80mg) was added into the aqueous solution of NH-MoO3-x (80mL, 1mg mL -1 ) at room temperature. After stirring for4h, the BSA-modified  NH-MoO3-x  (denoted  as  NH-MoO3-x@BSA)  was  obtained  by  high-speed centrifugation (12000rpm), and followed by washing with DI water for several times. Finally, the NH-MoO3-x@BSA was stored at4 °C for further usage.

Characterizations. A  transmission  electron  microscope  (JEOL  JEM-2100F)  was  used  to record TEM images. Cs -corrected transmission electron microscopes FEI TEAM1and JEOL ARM200F were used to take atomic-resolution STEM images. The SEM images were obtained by a scanning electron  microscope  (ZEISS Sigma500, USA). Powder XRD patterns were measured on a Bruker D8diffractometer (German), in which a Cu Kα (λ=1.54178 Å) is used as the X-ray source. XPS spectra were recorded on a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha+ instrument and  calibrated  by  using  the  C1s  peak  as  the  reference.  A  PerkinElmer  Lambda950

UV/VIS/NIR spectrometer was used to measure the absorption spectra. A planetary ball mill (QM-3SP04) was used for the ball milling treatment. The concentration of Mo elements of samples was detected by an inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS, Thermo Elemental  X7).  A  Bruker  electron  spin  resonance  (ESR)  spectroscopy  a300was  used  to monitor the signals of oxygen vacancies and Mo (V) with1.0G modulation amplitude of Xray band (9.85GHz), and100kHz modulation magnetic field under the100k.

Photothermal effect and photothermal conversion efficiency. 1mL of NH-MoO3-x@BSA aqueous solutions with different concentrations were irradiated by a NIR-II laser (1064nm, 1.0W cm -2 ) for10min, and a thermal camera was used to record the temperature changes. As for the test of thermal stability, 1mL of NH-MoO3-x@BSA solution (200ppm) was irradiated by a1064nm laser (1.0W cm -2 ) for10min, and the solution was then cooled to room temperature. This process was repeated with five cycles. The photothermal effect of NH-MoO3-x@BSA (200ppm) under various laser power density irradiations (0.4, 0.6, 0.8and1.0W cm -2 ) were also measured in the same way.

To study the photothermal conversion effect, the NH-MoO3-x@BSA (200ppm) was irradiated using a NIR-II laser (1064nm, 1.0W cm -2 ) for10min, and followed by shutting off the NIR laser.  Meanwhile,  thermal  imager  was  exploited  to  detect  the  temperature  increasing  and decreasing.

Peroxidase-like  activity  and  kinetic  assay. Peroxidase  (POD)-catalysed  oxidation  of colourless3,3',5,5'- tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) can form blue oxidized TMB (oxTMB) with H2O2as the electron acceptor. The POD-like activity of NH-MoO3-x@BSA was detected using TMB and H2O2as substrates in HAc-NaAc buffer solution (0.1M). A microplate reader was used to monitor the absorbance of the color solution, oxTMB with the maximum absorption peak at652nm and a certain reaction time.

To prove whether the POD-like activity of NH-MoO3-x@BSA can catalyze and decompose the low concentration of  H2O2that was near the level in the tumor microenvironment (TME). Various  concentrations  of  H2O2 (from0to2mM)  were  added  into  the  HAc-NaAc  buffer solution (pH = 5.0) containing NH-MoO3-x@BSA (50ppm) and TMB (1mM) at37o C. The temperature-dependent  POD-like  activity  of  NH-MoO3-x@BSA  was  evaluated  at  different temperature (from20to65o C) in the HAc-NaAc buffer solution (pH = 5.0) containing H2O2 (1mM) and TMB (1mM). In addition, the POD-like activity of NH-MoO3-x@BSA (50ppm) was further explored in the different pH values of HAc-NaAc buffer solution containing H2O2 (1mM) and TMB (1mM).

For  each  H2O2concentration  or  TMB  concentration,  the  initial  reaction  rates  ( V0 )  were calculated from the absorbance changes by Beer-Lambert Law (Equation3) (  =39000M -1cm1for  oxTMB).  The  rates  were  plotted  versus  H2O2concentration  and  then  fitted  by  the

Michaelis-Menten curves (Equation4). Furthermore, Lineweaver-Burk plot (Equation5) was used for measuring the Km and Vmax . Among them, the Km value is called the Michaelis constant, Vmax  is  the  reaction  rate  when  the  enzyme  is  saturated  with  the  substrate,  and  [S]  is  the concentration of the substrate.

Measurement  of  catalase-like  activity. The  catalase  (CAT)-like  activity  of  NH-MoO3x@BSA was evaluated at25o C by measuring the oxygen production with a dissolved oxygen instrument (JPSJ-605F). Specifically, 0.5mL of H2O2 (10mM) was added into a HAc-NaAc buffer (8.5mL, pH = 6.5 / 7.4), followed by adding1mL of MoO3 (50ppm) or NH-MoO3x@BSA (50ppm) solution. The oxygen concentration was immediately detected and the data was  continuously  recorded  for20min.  Moreover,  we  further  evaluated  the  effect  of  the irradiation by a1064nm laser on the oxidase (OXD)-like activity of NH-MoO3-x@BSA.

Measurement of oxidase-like  activity. Oxidase  (OXD-like)  activity  of  nanomaterials  can promote the convertion of H2O2into ion radicals (·O2). The OXD-like activity of MoO3/ NHMoO3-x@BSA was then detected by ESR spectroscopy using DMPO as a spin trap. 2 μL of DMPO was added into DMSO control (1mL), DMSO (1mL) + H2O2 (1 μL, 30%), DMSO (1mL) + MoO3 / NH-MoO3-x@BSA (1mg), and DMSO (1mL) + MoO3 / NH-MoO3-x@BSA (1mg) + H2O2 (1 μL, 30%), respectively, and measured immediately by ESR at room temperature.

H2O2consumption with NH-MoO3@BSA. The Ti(SO4)2assay was performed to evaluate the POD-  and  OXD-like  activity  of  NH-MoO3-x@BSA  by  monitoring  the  changes  of  H2O2

concentration. Typically, 25  L of H2O2 (2mM) and25  L NH-MoO3-x@BSA (400ppm) were added into HAc-NaAc buffer (pH = 6.5or7.4) at37 ℃. The mixture was added to Ti(SO4)2solution (100  L) that was obtained by adding24 % Ti(SO4)2 (1.33mL) and H2SO4 (8.33mL) into the DI water (50mL) and maintained for30min. The product (Ti-O2) was detected by the record of the UV absorbance at405nm.

Biocompatibility of NH-MoO3-x@BSA. The in vitro cytotoxicity of NH-MoO3-x@BSA was measured using CCK-8assay in mouse breast cancer cells (4T1cells) and cervical cancer cell line (HeLa cells) as well as H2O2low expressed cells Human Umbilical Vein Endothelial Cells (HUVEC). The cells were incubated in96-well plates (6000cells per well, 100  L of DMEM medium containing10% FBS and1% penicillin-streptomycin solution) at37o C in a humid atmosphere of5% CO2for24h. The NH-MoO3-x@BSA at serial concentrations (0, 3.12, 6.25, 12.5, 25, 50, 100, 200, and400ppm) were added to the above medium. As for the photothermal toxicity  of  NH-MoO3@BSA, various concentrations of NH-MoO3-x@BSA were calculated with4T1cells under a NIR-II laser (1064nm, 0.8W cm -2 ) for10min. After incubation for1day, 10  L of CCK-8solution in serum-free was added into every well. After incubating for further1h, the relative intensities of all wells at450nm were monitored using a microplate reader with the6repeats in each group.

Killing of tumor cells. 4T1cells (3 × 10 4cells / well) were pre-calculated in24-well plates for1day. And these cells were divided into four groups: (i) control, (ii) Laser, (iii) NH-MoO3x@BSA, (iv) NH-MoO3-x@BSA + Laser. Thereafter, NH-MoO3-x@BSA (200ppm) was added into groups (iii) and (iv), and the fresh medium was used as control. After culturing for6h, the medium placed under a NIR-II laser (1064nm, 0.8W cm -2 ) for10min in the laser groups. Subsequently, the medium was carefully removed and washed with cell culture PBS for several

times, and further calculated with CA-PI for another15min. Finally, the fluorescent images of cells were recorded by a fluorescence microscope (Olympus X73).

In vitro cytotoxicity assays under hypoxic condition. The4T1cells were seeded in96-well plates  at  a  cell  density  of5×10 3 /per  well  and  incubated  under  hypoxic  condition  for24h. Hypoxic condition was achieved by incubating cells in an anoxic environment (N2/CO2/O2 = 94/5/1in volume ratio) in an incubator. Different concentrations of NH-MoO3-x@BSA (0, 25, 50, 100, 200, 400 μg mL -1 ) were then added to the96-well plates and incubated for another6h. Then the plates were irradiated with1064nm laser (0.8W cm -2 , 10min per well). Finally, the cytotoxicity was tested with CCK-8reagent after incubating for18h.

ROS detection in cells. We used the typical probe DCFH-DA to evaluate the intracellular ROS. Simply, 4T1cells (2 × 10 4each well) were seeded in confocal dish and calculated with acidified DMEM supplemented with10% FBS. After pre-culturing  for24h,  the  cells  were  further incubated  with200ppm   of  NH-MoO3-x@BSA (with or without100  M of H2O2, with or without irradiation by a1064nm laser at the power density of0.8W cm -2for10min) for another12h. Then, the medium was removed and the cells were washed with PBS for several times. The serum-free DMEM containing DCFH-DA (10  M) and10  L Hoechst33342 (400ppm) was added into the dish and cultured for another20min at37o C in dark. After removing medium and washing with PBS for three times, the fluorescence imaging of4T1cells was recorded by a confocal microscopy laser scanning (CLSM, Nikon A1).

In vivo anti-tumor effect. The4T1tumor model was established by hypodermic injection with4T1cells into the right hind legs of Female BALB/c mice (six weeks old). All procedures used in  this  experiment  were  compliant  with  the  Chinese  Association  for  Laboratory  Animal Sciences  and  the  protocols  approved  by  the  Key  Laboratory  for  Biomedical  Effects  of Nanomaterials and Nanosafety, Institute of High Energy Physics. 4T1tumor-bearing BALB/c

mice were assigned into four groups (six mice each): (1) control; (2) only laser (1064nm, 0.8W cm -2 , 10min); (3) NH-MoO3-x@BSA; (4) NH-MoO3-x@BSA + laser (1064nm, 0.8W cm -2 , 10min). The mice can be carried out for in vivo experiments when its tumor size reaches around75mm3 . Tumor volume = (tumor length) × (tumor width) 2 /2. Relative tumor volumes were calculated as Vt/V0 (Vt is the tumor volume monitored after starting treatment, V0is the tumor volume when the treatment was initiated). The mice were intratumoral injected with PBS or NH-MoO3-x@BSA solution (5mg/kg). After12h, the tumor region was irradiated by1064nm laser.  The  tumor  volumes  and  weight  of  mice  were  recorded  every  day.  These  mice  were executed at day19to obtain the major organs and tumors for histological examination. Main organ  section  (heart,  liver,  spleen,  lung  and  kidney)  and  tumors  were  stained  with  Ki-67, hematoxylin and eosin (H&E). As for the metabolism of Mo in various tissues after19days of treatment, the appropriate samples (major organs and tumors) in group1 (PBS control) and group4 (NH-MoO3-x@BSA + laser) were dissolved in nitric acid, and then measured on ICPMS using the online internal standard method.

In vivo photothermal imaging . After injecting NH-MoO3-x@BSA or PBS for12h, the tumor was irradiated with a NIR-II laser (1064nm, 0.8W cm -2 ) for10min. Meanwhile, the thermal imager was exploited to detect the temperature increasing and decreasing in tumor sites.

Blood  hematology  and  biochemistry  analysis. After  post-treatment  for19-days,  blood samples were collected and then treated with anticoagulant (Na-EDTA) for further hematology analysis. Moreover, another residual blood (500 μL) of mice in each group was refrigerated at4 °C for4h, and followed by centrifugation to yield the blood plasma for biochemistry analysis. Blood  routine  and  blood  biochemistry  tests  indicators  were  evaluated  at  the  Animal Department  of  Peking  University  Medical  Laboratory.  Blood  index:  neutrophil  (GR), monocyte  count  (MO),  lymphocyte  count  (LY),  white  blood  cell  count  (WBC),  mean

corpuscular  hemoglobin  (MCH),  mean  corpuscular  volume  (MCV),  hematocrit  (HCT), thrombocytocrit (PCT), platelet distribution width (PDW), red blood cell count (RBC), red cell volume  distribution  width  (RDW)  and  platelet  (PLT).  Blood  biochemistry  index:  alanine aminotransferase (ALT), aspartate aminotransferase (AST), urea nitrogen (BUN), creatinine (CREA) and alkaline phosphatase (ALP).

The  low-magnification  transmission  electron  microscope  (TEM)  image  presents  a  similar morphology with the SEM image (Figure S1a).
